turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Number 152, Thomas W. Nelson and Arthur Globe, Petitioners, versus County of Los Angeles et al. Mr. Wirin.

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: May it please the Court. The petition here involves the validity of a discharge by Los Angeles County of two of its employees and initially very broadly stated the question as whether or not the discharge was that these employees were so arbitrary as to violate the due process guarantee in the Fourteenth Amendment. I think perhaps it maybe helpful if I would state to Your Honors in capsule form and very summarily the factual situation or the basis upon which the discharges were made. It is our position that the discharge -- the discharge of both petitioners by the County was solely because of their appearance under subpoena before a committee of Congress, the House Committee on Un-American Activities upon which appearance both defendants refused to answer certain questions pertaining to affiliation, pertaining to a communist affiliation upon -- based upon the Fifth Amendment as well as the First. It is our further contention that the inquiry conducted by the federal agency, House Committee on Un-American Activities was wholly unrelated to fitness for employment to loyalty in connection with fitness for employment by the County. And our further contention that both the petitioners showed the utmost candor and cooperation so far as their employer was concerned by answering all questions pertaining to their opinion and affiliation when propounded to them by the employer. And that therefore, the rationale if not the decision in Slochower against the Board accompanied by the gloss in Beilan against the Board and in Lerner versus Casey. Results in a conclusion, this is our plain that the discharges here were arbitrary. Let me put the problem a little bit differently. In terms of a statute of California, the validity of which as applied we are challenging here. That statute appears in the petitioners -- in the brief of petitioners, the white brief, on pages 2 and 3. These petitioners were discharged by the County under this statute. And as I say, as enforced against these petitioners, it is our contention that the statute violates due process. The statute appears, as I see on pages 2 and 3. I shall, in a moment, refer particular to page 3 of the last paragraph of the statute about the middle of the page. But let me tell Your Honors what the statute provides. It is a -- it is divisible in two parts, and I make the distinction and the division because we think it’s important to our case. This statute imposes an obligation upon all public employees in California to answer certain questions pertaining to affiliation and requires their dismissal if they do not comply with that mandate. It requires such employees to answer such questions when asked by the governing agency, governing body that appears on page 2. That portion of the statute is not an issue here because as I have already said, these petitioners answered all questions asked by the governing agency and fulfilled every requirement of any kind imposed upon them by the governing agency. But the statute goes one step further and it is in that regard that we are here. The statute additionally imposes an obligation upon all public employees in -- by any governmental agency in California. When appearing was subpoenaed before a committee -- a committee or subcommittee of Congress that appears, Your Honors, at page 2 where the Government Code is being quoted at about the sixth to seventh line of the quotation in the middle of the page.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Excuse me -- forgive me for interrupting you.

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: Indeed.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: It started up by saying -- by dealing it's relevant to give the -- what you call in capsule from the situation of the fact -- the fact of this case.

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: Yes, sir. I intend to develop the facts --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: You are now arguing as though we had nothing before us except the statute.

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: Oh, no, Your Honor. I merely now calling Your Honors' attention to the text of the statute and then I would like and I maybe want to take some time, if I may, to discuss the factual situation and then apply the statute to the factual situation in this case. And I am not -- we are not challenging the statute on its face. We are challenging the statute as applied. In a moment, I shall discuss the application to these cases. At the moment, I am merely just tell -- telling you --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: What then --

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: -- what the statute provides so you know what I'm talking about as I move along.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: That enlightened me. But I would get more -- I would get -- I could easily -- more easily in my mind apply the statute before I knew the fact for having the abstract statute --

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: Will you -- will you forgive me then. Let me just call your attention --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: You're the lawyer not I.

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: Pardon me.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: I say you're the lawyer who is arguing this case and not I. So go ahead your own way.

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: Well, all I want to say at this moment is that the statute in addition to requiring a person to answer before the governmental agency of a State also require an answer -- require the employees to answer questions propounded by Congressional Committee. And then and -- and after that, I should be done with the stays of it. Your Honors will note on page 3 --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: I -- do I gather from what you said that if California had to declare your judgment procedures, I assume it has under which you could have gone before the California Court, assuming you're outstanding there, to get declaration is for the constitutionality of the statute and they had to stay there, if I understand you, you would not be coming here finding any difficulty which the stating the statute in abstract.

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: Please, I --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: -- Your Honor understand me correctly --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: All right.

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: -- completely. I am attacking the statute only as applied to these petitioners and what I want to say now, I'm -- think -- I must say now is that this statute provides that refusal to answer questions before a committee of the Congress on any ground whatsoever and that -- that's in -- now on page 3 in the middle paragraph shall constitute ground toward dismissal. Now, of course, the California courts and -- and the respondent read the phrase on any ground whatsoever as we also are compelled to read it, it includes refusal to answer before a Congressional Committee based upon the Fifth Amendment, that's a ground. And -- and therefore, the courts below have held that the refusal of the petitioners to answer questions falls within the statute even though there isn't any question of what the refusal of the petitioners to answer questions was based upon their invocation of the Fifth Amendment before the Committee. Now, let me turn to some of the facts.

turns.name: JOHN M. HARLAN, II
turns.text: And the difference between the statute and the Slochower statute, as I understand it, is that the Slochower statute in terms referred to the Fifth Amendment and this one doesn't on its face.

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: That's --

turns.name: JOHN M. HARLAN, II
turns.text: Is that substantial --

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: That's substantially correct. Of course, the Slochower statute, if I may recall your -- didn't talk about the Fifth Amendment, it was a state statute. And it talked about the invocation of the privilege --

turns.name: JOHN M. HARLAN, II
turns.text: Correct.

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: -- but Your Honors substantially correct. But what we're saying is that our statute, I was going to say, is worst in a way than the statute of Slochower because of the comprehensive basis upon which an employee must be dismissed on any ground whatsoever, but in any event that we're mistaken about that. We say that as applied in this case, this statute has the same effect as the application of the statute in Slochower because it's applied in this case. The sole reason for the dismissal of these employees is the fact of their invocation of the privilege against self-incrimination when appearing before a federal agency invoking a federal privilege before such federal agency in an inquiry by that agency totally unrelated to fitness for employment by the County of Los Angeles. Now, then, it is conceded and there is no dispute but what these petitioners upon their appearance before this Committee properly invoked the Fifth Amendment. The Committee finally acknowledged that the Amendment was property invoked and the County here concedes that the Amendment was properly invoked by these petitioners.

turns.name: CHARLES E. WHITTAKER
turns.text: Would it be legally (Inaudible)

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: Yes. I will accept that settling. I think properly it goes a little -- includes legally and goes a step further, suggest kind of a moral propriety but this case doesn't involve moral so I'll -- I'll talk about this case and not the formal one that my partner's argued. Now, a brief word as to the course of these cases through the California courts and then I must go down to discussing the factual showing made by the petitioners from which we ask this Court to draw a conclusion of that, the action of the County was arbitrary. Petitions for writ of mandate were filed in the Los Angeles Superior Court in behalf of the petitioners except for petitions. The Los Angeles Superior Court ordered the writ of mandate to issue in behalf of one of the petitioner, the petitioner Globe. The petitioner Globe under the classified system in California and by the County was a probationary employee. He was summarily dismissed because of his having refused to answer questions on the grounds I've indicated by the County. He was afforded no hearing of any kind. And the Superior Court took the position that that violated the -- the mandate of the rule in the Slochower case to summarily discharge a person for having invoked a privilege before a federal agency and ordered the writ of mandate to issue. And after the petitioner Nelson, the petitioner was a -- is a permanent employee in the classified system of the County and he was entitled to a hearing and he received it. In a moment, I shall tell Your Honors about that hearing but the Superior Court ruled adversely to him. And then at each losing side, petitioner Nelson on the one hand, the County of Los Angeles in the Globe case took appeals and the District Court of Appeal upheld the discharges as to both petitioners. We then filed a petition for hearing in the Supreme Court of California, the highest court of the State. It precedes somewhat analogous to the -- to certiorari proceedings here, the Supreme Court of California granting a review in its discretion. The California Supreme Court divided narrowly the seven-judge court, four justices voted against the hearing, three justices, Chief Justice Gibson and Justice Traynor and Carter voted in favor of a hearing but it wasn't enough and so a hearing was denied and now we're here.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Who was the third that you --

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: Justice Clark -- I don't quite I say -- Justice Carter, Jesse Carter who is --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Carter.

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: Carter who has passed -- no passed away. Justice Jesse Carter and Justice -- Justice Traynor and the Chief Justice, Chief Justice Gibson voted for hearing. Now, I think also we will not be questioned but that the sole reason given by the employer that the County of Los Angeles for the discharge was the appearance of the petitioners before the House Committee. I think it can -- it will not be disputed but what the Civil Service Commission of Los Angeles County which accorded a hearing to Mr. Nelson in its findings ultimately against him made a finding that he was discharged because of his having invoked the privilege of the Fifth Amendment before the House Committee on Un-American Activities.

turns.name: CHARLES E. WHITTAKER
turns.text: May I ask you right this?

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: Yes, sir.

turns.name: CHARLES E. WHITTAKER
turns.text: At that hearing, was Mr. Nelson re-asked by his employer with the County three questions he had declined to answer before the Congressional Committee?

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: At that hearing, he was not re-asked the questions or any question pertaining to or in anyway in -- in -- connected with the question.

turns.name: CHARLES E. WHITTAKER
turns.text: Would -- one -- one more question.

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor.

turns.name: CHARLES E. WHITTAKER
turns.text: Would you tap then a defense to this discharge proceeding if he had answered -- been asked and answered those questions at that place?

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: Oh, yes. And it is our claim, and I shall get to -- I've cited quite immediately, as to the showing which he affirmatively made at that hearing pertaining to his loyalty.

turns.name: CHARLES E. WHITTAKER
turns.text: Well, I thought that the statute said he -- it wouldn't be a defense, really, that he must only explain why he answered as he did to the Congressional Committee and that in fact to answer fully to this Civil Service Commission the very questions he had refused to answer to the House Committee would not be a defense. Am I right or wrong?

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: Well, I think Your Honor is more right than I. And I think the statute certainly on its face and we have no construction of any -- by the -- by the courts of California to the contrary.

turns.name: CHARLES E. WHITTAKER
turns.text: Are you going in circle on the District Court decisions?

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: Oh, no.

turns.name: CHARLES E. WHITTAKER
turns.text: I notice meager of the cited decision which is by mean in the case of Davis against the University of Kansas City in 127 F.Supp., it has a good deal to do with this question 129 F.Supp.716.

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: Well, it's just how ignorance not to our -- it's responsible for him. Not that I disrespect for --

turns.name: CHARLES E. WHITTAKER
turns.text: No, no. I didn't mean -- not at all.

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: -- for decisions of any court. And we shall read it by tomorrow morning and may have something to say tomorrow morning about that if it's -- if it's helpful to it, well, I hope it is.

turns.name: JOHN M. HARLAN, II
turns.text: Can I --

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor.

turns.name: JOHN M. HARLAN, II
turns.text: -- interject one of the remarks you made. You say the statute has never been construed. Do you ever discussed the Mass case?

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: Yes, I'll discuss it right -- I haven't been -- I know by now.

turns.name: JOHN M. HARLAN, II
turns.text: I'll tell you exactly to save your trouble. Let me tell you what's in my mind.

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: Certainly.

turns.name: JOHN M. HARLAN, II
turns.text: The Mass case came down after Slochower and it came down after this client of yours who was discharged under this procedure. And as I read the hearing that he was given before the Civil Service Commission, it was not the kind of a hearing that the Mass case with its gloss on the statute would have required. And my question is whether in light of that later procedure required by the Mass case why this case shouldn't go back for a further hearing in light of the requirements of the Mass case.

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: Well --

turns.name: JOHN M. HARLAN, II
turns.text: In other words why we have to reach the question that you're imposing now.

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: Well, yes. I -- I see some problems as follows. The Mass case was called to the attention of the District Court of Appeal when we had our appeal before that Court. And the District Court of Appeal in our case refused to read the Mass case. As -- as Your Honor has just -- just suggested and has refused to consider the Mass case as even requiring any kind of a hearing for -- for Globe. He --

turns.name: JOHN M. HARLAN, II
turns.text: Globe is the temporary.

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: For Globe is temporary.

turns.name: JOHN M. HARLAN, II
turns.text: He (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: And -- and the District Court of Appeal ruled that the hearing accorded to the petitioner Nelson was in accordance with the -- with the Mass case. Now, we disagreed with that and expressed our disagreement to the Supreme Court of California. And all we could get out of the Supreme Court of California on that issue was -- was three votes. So that we do have a situation where the California courts, first, the District Court of Appeal and then the Supreme Court itself by a majority vote seems to take the view that the requirements which the Supreme Court of California suggest that are imposed in Mass have been met here. And therefore, I think, were properly here and I think that this matter should be resolved having -- having exhausted all of our avenues and courts in California should now be resolved by this Court.

turns.name: WILLIAM O. DOUGLAS
turns.text: I understand that. What they say about this case and the Mass case is one -- page 195 that is -- turns on the status, the temporary status of -- of Nelson.

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: Well, we think the District Court of Appeal was in error in two respects. In the first place --

turns.name: WILLIAM O. DOUGLAS
turns.text: But that's their distinction, was it?

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: That -- that's one distinction. On the other hand, the District Court of Appeal does not read the -- the decision of the Supreme Court in the Mass case as permitting us to make, including Nelson, to make a complete showing at this hearing with respect to his loyalty and as showing to the effect that the same questions which had been asked of him by the House Committee had been asked by the employer and that he had answered these questions with utmost candor completely and fully and had completely demonstrated his loyalty at the hearing which he was accorded --

turns.name: WILLIAM O. DOUGLAS
turns.text: That's was in the --

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: -- before the Court.

turns.name: WILLIAM O. DOUGLAS
turns.text: -- other ground for the -- their distinction in the Mass case.

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: Yes. And therefore, let me then come to the factual showing which was made by the petitioner Nelson at the hearing which he was accorded under California law, a showing which we say discloses complete condor to the employer. Submitting full information concerning his opinions and his associations to the employer followed by the acceptance of his -- of that information by the employer, and we think a recognition by the employer that Nelson was a fit employee. And when I say fit, I mean fitness including loyalty in any -- in any meaning that that word passed or has been given by this Court. Now, here is what happened before the Commission and here is the evidence that he offered to the Civil Service Commission urging that his discharge should not be made final. He became a temporary employee according to the record in 1949. The moment he became an employee of the County, it was necessary for him to begin to comply with an elaborate investigatory and check loyalty system which the County of Los Angeles had been in effect a system which this Court passed upon in the opinion written by Mr. Justice Frankfurter in Parker against the County of Los Angeles, a system which this Court upheld. And he took then a -- what I would call colloquially because it's a shorthand phrase, a -- a loyalty ought but a particular kind of loyalty oath imposed and acquired by the County of Los Angeles. That loyalty oath was not only an -- the conventional oath of support of the Constitution. It had affidavits and it contained an affidavit to the effect that he did not advocate the destruction or govern by violence and that he had not done so, that he did not belong to any organization that so advocates, and that he had not done so, and he was required to list such organizations as he belong to the did and this -- this of course he did not do because he was not a member of any such organization. In addition to that, that list and Mr. Justice Frankfurter had the entire oath attached as an appendix to the opinion of this Court in the Parker case which we referred to in our brief. In addition to that, he was required to state whether or not he was a member of or had directly or indirectly supported any of the following organizations. And then there -- there was a list of 142 organizations consisting of some of the organizations that had appeared on the Attorney General's list of the interstates and a large number about this that a special committee set up by the Los Angeles County Board of Supervisors had considered sufficiently suspect so as to make inquiry as to membership. And he signed that oath and that affidavit and in -- and checked one of those 142 organizations indicating that he was -- had been a member of it, namely, the United Public -- the United Federal Workers. Now, upon his employment, he was expressly questioned pertaining to his loyalty, pertaining to his affiliation, pertaining to his membership in this organization. And Your Honors will find if Your Honors would look at -- for instance, page 58 of the record. It isn't the most -- it -- it is a long -- it is a statement in longhand made by Dorothy E.White as you will see on page 58 if you'll turn the page around. Well, it is an interview by a representative of the County of Los Angeles with the petitioner Nelson. In the course of which he asked him about his membership in the United Public -- United Federal Workers and he, with complete candor, stated he had been a member for two years from 1942 to 1944. And Your Honors will note particularly the less legible portions of this document on page 59 in which he concludes with the statement in -- and this is in her handwriting and her statement that Nelson had said that he was definitely democratic and uphold and against all communistic principles the inter lineation of is by the representative of the County not by the petitioner. And so having -- having told the County about that affiliation and satisfied the County with respect to his loyalty, he was employed.

turns.name: JOHN M. HARLAN, II
turns.text: This was before the Un-American Activities (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: Oh, this was -- this was in 1949, Un-American Activity is -- is 1956, this is years before. Now, Your Honor, this is -- if you'll excuse me, this is just the beginning of it as to what I'm going to say in connection with the showing he made. This was in 1949. It was --

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: Is this -- is this when he first got the job or --

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: First got the job in 19 --

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: This is on his job after application or -- or part --

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: Well -- he -- he first was hired in 1949. And that appears in the record 44 -- at -- at 40 -- 44 and 45. But, Your Honor, he then resigned for personal reasons without any relationship to loyalty and reapplied again in April 1952. And when he reapplied, and I think I -- I -- it wasn't clear in my explanation. When he reapplied, he then had to take another loyalty oath, the same one. And he then indicated again that he would have been a member of this organization and he was at that time in 1952 that he was interviewed and questioned and he made an explanation. And this statement by Ms. or Mrs. White indicates that he had made the same statement about membership in this organization in 1949 when he was first employed. And so, he was employed in 1952 after having passed this test. And the record shows that at or about the time, he -- he filled out a questionnaire, an elaborate questionnaire in the course of which he was asked with respect to former employments. And he indicated, for instance -- pardon me. For instance in the record at 57, he indicated that he had been employed in a civilian capacity by the United States Army and he indicated on -- at 57 concerning that employment. And at 63, page 63 at the same time in April 1952, perhaps this is a little better than the former one.

turns.name: WILLIAM O. DOUGLAS
turns.text: What is that page?

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: Page 63. He had to indicate who all his employers were or whether he was dismissed or resigned and the reasons for dismissal. And Your Honors will find, for instance, in the -- towards the bottom right hand column with respect to the Army that he was dismissed. And then in the extreme bottom left hand, he indicates that he was dismissed from United States Army giving -- giving the date. And a little bit later, in a questionnaire which he had filled out, he indicated that he had been dismissed from the United States Army because, as he put it, this appears in the record at 74, at the very bottom of the page of 74 towards the left -- left side of the page, he indicates that he was dismissed by the United States Army for alleged -- as the alleged violation of Public Law 808. And later on, he was asked about that and he indicated that it was Public Law 808 of the 77th Congress. And that's the statute which provides for discharge of persons in the employee of -- the agencies of the Government for security reasons and -- and provides for -- for summary dismissal. So he told these things to the County, the County knew these things, and the County thereafter gave him promotions, gave him increase in compensation, gave him ratings, for instance, of -- of excellent.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: What is the relevancy of this -- the legal question?

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: Well, the relevancy to the legal question is this. It is our contention that under the comprehensive loyalty check system invoked and used by the County as to all of its employees including this -- this petitioner, the County knew all about him, made various inquiries from him, pertaining to his loyalty, pertaining to his affiliation, all of which with respect to none of which he disclosed any information, with respect to all of which he showed complete candor. And --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Did you take judicial notice of that?

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: No. It's in -- this is in the record.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: What?

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: Oh, no, not at all. This is a matter which is in the record.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: You mean it was adjudicated that although the truthful statement --

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: Well --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- the mere fact that a man and a questionnaire doesn't need for the answers of the suit, does it?

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: No. But we think that -- for instance, the statement which Ms. or Mrs. White represented the County made as a result of an interview from him is some evidence from which an inference can be drawn that the employer at least in that situation was satisfied with his fitness. But now --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: But that --

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: -- but now --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- that precludes the County from reexamining himself.

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: Oh, not at all. And if --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Can I ask -- I'd like to echo some (Inaudible)

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: What is the relevance?

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: Well, the relevance of the matter is this. We fit -- we say that we assume that under Beilan and Lerner, the County was entitled to have answers to the very questions which the petitioner refused to answer before this House Committee. That the County was entitled to had answers to those questions. And that we say the County had received answers to those questions. And that when the County therefore, summarily and discharged the petitioners solely because of their appearance before the Committee --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: But is there -- is that the record solely pure?

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: That's precisely the record, Your Honors. There is -- there --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: So Justice Whittaker put a question to you to which you say give some label. And to which you said he was more accurately in stating what the record discloses than you were.

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: Well, I have said that it is probable, that a fair construction of the California statute is to the effect that even though an employee answered all questions of the employer. He nonetheless is subject to discharge if he doesn't answer such questions put by the House Committee and -- and I would -- I would agree with that. And this is what I -- I'm agreeing with.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: This is the sequence of events. He -- he was asked questions, he was put through this so-called loyalty procedure (Inaudible) and he made discloses to satisfy the inquiring officer at the time subsequent even before -- before a Congressional Committee. He refused to answer questions. Thereupon, the County took another look at his words.

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: No. This is where we defer. We -- we do not say the County took another look in that it --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Well --

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: -- made inquiry of him --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: No, no, no. But it said a new situation was created by his refusal to answer questions. And then it put him to explaining or justifying that before the Civil Service Commission. Is that an accurate statement?

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: Well, no. I wouldn't say it was inaccurate.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Read correctly --

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: It isn't, I would say this. This being so when he appeared before the Civil Service Commission, he then, as we claim, took on the challenge as to whether or not he was fit and loyal by offering evidence pertaining to his fitness and loyalty by offering his personnel file which showed not only these matters which I have adverted to, but which showed that as of late -- as late as January 1956, that's three months prior to his appearance before the Committee. At that time, there had been an inquiry made in connection with his affiliation and his loyalty and that he had been checked as -- as satisfactory.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: That means the implication of that answer of yours wherein he --that the County -- that was res judicata and they could make further inquiries.

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: Not at all.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: They couldn't make further inquiries, to your phrase, the -- the challenge could be taken up by him to explain why, although he made full disclosure according to his -- your view, would satisfy the inquiring of parties when he came down here, he -- he didn't.

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: Well, let me --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: -- let me answer, Your Honor -- I -- I --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Are those -- are those the facts?

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: I -- I think not quite for this -- with this -- because of this exception. Had the County, after these employees had appeared before the Committee and refused to answer, called in these employees and said, “Now, look, you have not answered certain questions of the House Committee. We, in order to determine your fitness, now want to ask you some questions.” And had the County asked the same questions which the employees had refused to answer before the Congressional Committee and had the employees refused to answer those questions to the county. In other words, had the County really taken a new -- a new look in the light of what had transpired before the Committee, that would not be our case.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: But -- but the difference between you and me your correction is merely saying the County should have done something which, all I know, I might agree with you, would be a more rational method. What the County did do to -- to act without serving inquiry upon the stated answer before the Congressional Committee and then put him to an explanation for doing so, is that correct?

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: All right.

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: But we say that he made the explanation --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Well, he made --

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: -- by -- by offering evidence with respect to his loyalty to the County by offering his personnel record which showed that he had answered all these questions to the County before at --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: I will put to you again.

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Your claim then is that that res judicata and the County couldn't go about it in the frank movement, namely, you go before the Civil Service Commission and satisfied them that you have good reason for withholding answers before the Congressional Committee. Isn't that it?

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: Mr. Justice Frankfurter, not quite for this reason. The first -- first place --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: (Voice Overlap) fact --

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: -- in the first place, I'm not claiming that -- I'm not claiming the doctrine or of any concept of res judicata.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: I'm not using the word but you're claiming it in effect.

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: I am claiming that this case is like Slochower because I am claiming that where an employer has information concerning an employee and has it as -- as recently as January prior to the April when he's discharged. And then the employees called before a federal inquiry, conducting an inquiry not related to offense. And then the employer summarily discharges the employee solely for that reason. I am claiming that that's a violation of the principle which was laid down in Slochower --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: He would --

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: -- that -- may I just --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Now --

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: -- that an -- that an --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: (Voice Overlap) -- summarily discharged. That's my point. He was not summarily discharged.

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: Well -- well, he was summarily discharged in the first instance and then he was given an opportunity for a hearing in the second instance. Now, as to --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: (Voice Overlap) in the first instance --

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: Well --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- he was not summarily discharged without having been given the opportunity to explain why he didn't answer before the House Committee what you're saying he so abundantly answered to the lady who inquired him.

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: Well, my --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Where is the --

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: Sure.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: -- where is the order which would show us whether or not -- what --what the discharge is? Is it in writing?

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: It is. I'll give it to you.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Where is it?

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: I'll give it to you in a moment.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Is it the one on page 103?

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: Let's look at 115.

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: Yes, 115. Yes. The order of discharge in the first instance appears at the bottom of page 115. And contained in that order are merely recitals of their refusal of the employee to answer questions before the House Committee and --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Where is that?

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: This is at the bottom of page 115. It's --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: It reads Exhibit A to respondent's return.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: May 2nd.

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: Dated May 2nd.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Well, now --

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: And -- and if Your Honor will look at page --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Is that the only order that's --

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: Well, if Your Honor --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: If you look at the --

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: If Your Honor will look at page 116 at the bottom, you will see that --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: At the bottom, you will see the recital which is made by the employer constituting the factual basis, basis for the discharge. But Mr. Justice Black, Mr. Justice Frankfurter is quite right that after Nelson received this notice, he was entitled to a hearing and he received a hearing.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: But where is that?

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: Now, that hearing appears -- begins at page 1.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: That's the hearing after this order.

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: Hearing after --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: -- this order with -- as to whether or not the discharge should be made final.

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: And he was notified in this order of his right to request a hearing (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: Oh, yes. He re -- he requested a hearing and he received a hearing. And as a result of the hearing, the Commission positive its -- its ruling in supporting the discharge on the ground that Nelson had refused to answer questions invoking the Fifth Amendment.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Where is that?

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: 103. In other words, the ground on which he was discharged, you say is set out on page 103 --

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: Well --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: -- after the (Inaudible).

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: Yes.103, 104, 105, perhaps one -- beginning with 104, indeed what the -- what the Commission does is to quote what happened -- the quote of the exact transcript of the proceeding before House Committee and you'll note, for instance, on page 104 --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: -- paragraph 7 that the -- that it says that the employee refused to answer these questions on the basis of the First Amendment supplemented by the Fifth Amendment of the United States Constitution. And then the Board continues to find that that -- that this -- this action by this employee before this Committee under these circumstances constituted insubordination under the California statute and was ground for and constituted the basis for the discharge.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Is refusal to answer the Congressional Committee.

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: Precisely. No -- no reason for his dismissal of --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: (Voice Overlap) now all the orders that were made in connection with this (Inaudible)

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: These are all the orders which were made in connection with his discharge by any administrative agency. The Los Angeles Superior Court ruled that the sole reason for the discharge was the appearance before the Committee and unification of the privilege.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: What page is that?

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: All right.

turns.name: CHARLES E. WHITTAKER
turns.text: (Inaudible) page -- beginning 115 (Inaudible)

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: Yes. This is the -- this is the discharge.

turns.name: CHARLES E. WHITTAKER
turns.text: The order.

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: This is the order. Then there was a review of the order before the -- before the Commission and the Commission confirmed the order. Now, at the -- the record at 123, Your Honor will find the ruling by the Superior Court. But -- but Your Honor -- excuse me for a moment. The Los Angeles Superior Court so construed the matter. If Your Honors will look at record 118.

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: Yes, and the beginning of 119.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: All right.

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: For instance the Court said in the -- in the last paragraph were in the record 118, in each of the cases before the Court, the petitioner was discharged from county employment upon the ground. What ground? Only one ground.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Where is that?

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: The ground -- this is the last paragraph on page 118. The ground that he had been guilty of insubordination and the violation of this section which we are challenging as applied.

turns.name: CHARLES E. WHITTAKER
turns.text: (Inaudible) as clear as the order itself (Inaudible)

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: I was trying to give Your Honor a few -- an -- a facetious remark, a -- a good opinion by this -- by a Superior Court --

turns.name: CHARLES E. WHITTAKER
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: -- judgement order. Now, then, I -- I do want to say one further word about -- about the record of the hearing before the Committee and that is, Mr. Justice Frankfurter will bear with me, if Your Honors would -- would look at page 96 of the record, you will find a document which we think has a special significance. Your Honors will note that this document on 96 purports to represent that Mr. Nelson's file was taken by the Sheriff's Office of Los Angeles County on January 11, 1956 on the upper right hand column and was examined a file which disclosed this matter of the employment by the petitioner by the Army as well as the interview by Dorothy E. White which I've already called to your attention.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Well, why is that relevant if they didn't discharge him for that ground?

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: Well, it's only relevant because of our -- our insistence, if you'll forgive me, upon our position that the County was at all times completely satisfied with the loyalty of this employee and that the -- and -- and we're trying to demonstrate beyond any peradventure of the doubt that the sole -- and perhaps we've already done it and now just bringing calls to -- to an unnecessary place that the sole reason for the discharge was because of the conduct of the petitioners before the Committee and not because of any relationship to --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Well, I don't quite understand. If that is the sole ground of dismissal as you it is --

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: -- the -- this Court, they proceed to (Inaudible) --

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: -- what difference does it make about what happened before isn't your legal question where they had a right to discharge him on the ground that they did.

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: Well, but, you see, the respondent takes the position that somehow or rather the conduct of these petitioners before the Committee is related to their loyalty to the County and to their fitness for employment.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Are you then --

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: And we are trying to negate that.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Well, did the Board or the Court find it?

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: No board and no court upon the discharge of either of the petitioners on the ground of lack of loyalty to the County, but solely on the ground that I've indicated.

turns.name: CHARLES E. WHITTAKER
turns.text: You're making an argument then, as I understand it, which a little bit anticipatory of what your adversary may have to say. Your point really is, as I understand it, if I don't understand it I want to understand it, that this man was discharged as the order says, that's Nelson now, the order at 116 says, namely, for a refusal to answer these questions to the House Committee under the ordinance in question and the question presented to us is, is that result justified by that law on those facts? Isn't that our question?

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: Precisely.

turns.name: CHARLES E. WHITTAKER
turns.text: Now, we (Inaudible) are we?

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Was there any ground on which he refused to answer except for the Fifth Amendment?

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: He also said the First Amendment, but he always said the Fifth when he said the First.

turns.name: JOHN M. HARLAN, II
turns.text: Supposing he had -- simply he gone before the House Un-American Activities Committee to your own answer when no constitutional right or so, would the -- the California (Inaudible)

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: Well, I think perhaps so for the following reason.

turns.name: JOHN M. HARLAN, II
turns.text: Well, if they could.

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: Well, I -- I think -- I think perhaps so for the following reason, may I state the reason? This leads me to my -- to my next argument. It seems to us that where a discharge of a county employee would demonstrate this fitness is based because of his refusal to answer for federal agency upon invoking the Fifth Amendment. But certain constitutional considerations and broad policy considerations come into play which might not come into play if the refusal was merely obstinate and adamant and without the use of the privilege for these reasons. In the first place, the use of the privilege before a federal agency is, of course, a right guaranteed by the Constitution. This Court has said recently and for many years, it's one of the great privileges and treasures of our -- in our constitutional system. It is said that no inference of guilt shall -- shall fall as a result of the use of the privilege. And it is said it is available to the innocent as well as the guilty. Now, then, had these petitioners merely stubbornly refused to answer a Congressional Committee? You would not have come into play the -- what we think is an important and ultimately over balancing consideration in favor of these petitioners that all they did is what the Constitution of the United States told them they could do at a place where they had a full right to do it. And that therefore a discharge under such circumstances, whether intended by the County or not, has the inevitable respect of diluting or negating or seriously abridging the force and effect of this great constitutional privilege. And of course, I could have answered your question by saying we needn't come to that because that's not this case. But I do say --

turns.name: JOHN M. HARLAN, II
turns.text: Wait a minute. Is there anything in this record that indicates that they were drawing an inference of Communist activities, inference of guilt from his claim of privilege before the House American -- Un-American Activities Committee?

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: I -- I think not.

turns.name: JOHN M. HARLAN, II
turns.text: You think not.

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: No. I think that the -- the clear absolute unequivocal position which the County was taken was that the refusal to answer questions of the Federal Committee on any ground whatsoever constituted a ground for -- not constituted but made a -- it was a mandatory ground for dismissal under the statute and that --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Well, now, you make -- you state that in a way it causes distinction without any supposed (Inaudible) I thought you said that the only thing they -- the questions they asked him and he refused to answer was based on the Fifth Amendment.

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Now, you are saying that -- I understood you to say that on any ground whatever.

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: Well, the statute under which the County function --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: And your arguing as it find here.

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: Yes. The statute said on any ground whatsoever as applied here, the only ground upon which the defendants -- the petitioners now saying they relied is the -- and -- and it is considered by the County is the Fifth Amendment so that this statute broad on its face as narrowly applied to this case involved a discharge of county employees for having invoked the privilege before a federal agency of the Fifth Amendment.

turns.name: JOHN M. HARLAN, II
turns.text: Is your argument a due process argument or is it abridging immunity -- federal immunity? Which in --

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: Well --

turns.name: JOHN M. HARLAN, II
turns.text: -- your argument?

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: -- it's essentially a due process argument. We'd like to make also a privileges and immunities argument out of it but counsel has called our attention to the fact --

turns.name: JOHN M. HARLAN, II
turns.text: It isn't raised.

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: -- we didn't make that in the --

turns.name: JOHN M. HARLAN, II
turns.text: And --

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: -- state court. We raised it for the first time in this Court and this is embarrassing and so we don't press -- press the matter.

turns.name: JOHN M. HARLAN, II
turns.text: Well, your -- your due process argument then which you have to stand on is this. That although if he had claimed no federal rights at all but simply it refused to answer, California could've fire him without more.

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: Well --

turns.name: JOHN M. HARLAN, II
turns.text: But since he claimed his federal rights, they can't fire him as a matter of due process -- due process even though the record does not show and there is an expressed disclaimer in the record that they were drawing any unfavorable of -- inference of guilt or anything else from his claim of privilege, is the your argument?

turns.name: CHARLES E. WHITTAKER
turns.text: Is it really?

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: Well, it's one of my arguments. I think the -- the broader argument which I make --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: (Voice Overlap) due process, what do you press?

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: The -- the due process which this Court talked about in the Slochower case that a --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: What you're talking about? Fourteenth Amendment or federal immunity?

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: Of course. Well, I am not saying due process, I mean Fourteenth.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: I -- I mean that --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: (Voice Overlap) stated, I am not expert about that.

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: Well -- well --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Definite -- definite way in which you have to raise the constitutional --

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: Well --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: -- ground --

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: Well, I --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: (Voice Overlap) objection.

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: Our -- our claim is that an arbitrary dismissal by --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: And what was your objection in court order?

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: This was our -- this is our objection and throughout the California court.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: Well, yes, we have it in the record. Well, let's take the petition for rehearing. Our complaint is in -- is at page 106, a complaint -- the petition filed in Los Angeles Superior Court and you'll find our federal constitutional grounds listed on page 109 and they are that the discharge was arbitrary and violated due process. Now, we say that, we say, it violates due process and constitutes arbitrariness for an employer to be charge an employee for having refused to answer before a federal agency invoking the privilege against self-incrimination. Now, one -- one further --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Do you have -- do you have other (Inaudible) in the First Amendment?

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: Oh, yes. But -- distilling our various points and reducing them in this Court to -- to -- as --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: I -- I want to know what you raise because I'm interested in your point that whether or not if this is solely a discharge (Inaudible) the constitutional privilege. I want to know if I'm barred from considering it by your failure to object on the right ground.

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: Well, I -- let me just say this to Your Honor. There's no question but what -- this is our position that's county counsel can help us out, there is no question about what -- throughout the California courts. We took the position that a discharge of this petitioners for having refused to answer before this Committee, whereas the refusal was based on the Fifth Amendment was arbitrary and hence, was a denial of due process --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: As far as I'm concern, the Constitution doesn't say anything about arbitrary.

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: Well, the Slochower does.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: The Slochower does.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Yes, I understand --

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: And we think that's the umbrella which -- which covers us. And we think it's the umbrella which covers us for still -- for still another reason. Because in Slochower, this Court took the view that a person subpoenaed before a committee may sometimes find himself in -- in an ambiguous position as a result of which it is appropriate that he -- that he invoked the Fifth Amendment. And Mr. Justice, of course, I'm referring to the language of Mr. Justice Clark in that decision. I'm referring to similar language used by Mr. Justice Harlan in the -- in the Grunewald case as well as I think in -- in Lerner versus Casey. And I'm referring to somewhat similar language, and then I shall sit down by Dean Griswold in his talks on the Fifth Amendment in which he said that often a -- a witness appearing before a -- a Congressional Committee find himself under such circumstances where he may seek the Fifth Amendment as a sanctuary from --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Well, but you have -- one of your grounds for objection is with respect to the Fifth Amendment in that it bridges its right to be free from being a witness against himself.

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: That is --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: That is one of your grounds.

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: That's one of our grounds, yes. And what I'm developing now is that under these circumstances of this case, the petitioners' appearance before this Committee was an appearance which justified him in using the Fifth Amendment because at one point, the Chairman questioned whether he was properly invoking the Fifth Amendment where they hadn't waived it and at another point, remarks completely unflattering to the witness were made while photographers were present. And so we think this is precisely the kind of a case where the use of a Fifth Amendment by a witness before a committee, when that committee was not investigating his fitness for employment cannot be used as the sole reason for discharging him from county employment.

turns.name: JOHN M. HARLAN, II
turns.text: (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Are you -- before you sit down, Mr. --

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: Yes, sir.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- Wirin. You make no distinction between Nelson and Globe, do you in this case?

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: I -- we do in our briefs.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: In your briefs.

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: And our --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: (Voice Overlap) about it.

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: -- if I -- of course.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: All right.

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: The time of that (Inaudible).

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: But what -- where is the distinction?

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: -- because tremendous distinction of course. Globe was given no hearing, no opportunity --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: No, I don't --

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: -- to explain of any kind.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: (Voice Overlap) status of the distinction.

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: Well, we think the status of Globe that should not disqualify him from relief in this Court because we think Wieman versus Updegraff and Slochower against the Board drew no distinction between temporary employees and permanent employees. The employees in Slochower included both permanent employees.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Did they -- did the Court address itself to the problem and say there are no distinction?

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: Oh, no, the courts below took the position that there is a distinction.

turns.name: TOM C. CLARK
turns.text: Slochower was permanent.

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: Slochower was permanent. In Wieman, some of the employees were not. And in (Inaudible), of course, he was just an applicant for -- for a license and you --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Was the point taken into consideration?

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: Pardon me.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Is this Court address itself to the problem --

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: No.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- or merely take out that fact in the record?

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: We -- we dug -- we dug it out from the -- both the record and the opinion.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: Yes, of course. Yes.

turns.name: JOHN M. HARLAN, II
turns.text: Really, the essence of your position lies in that statute? There's no rational connection between the claim of privilege before a federal committee and a finding of insubordination as here, is -- is that it?

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: Where the Committee is not investigating fitness for employment --

turns.name: JOHN M. HARLAN, II
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: -- which should be the only consideration that -- that should prompt the employer.

turns.name: JOHN M. HARLAN, II
turns.text: Is that -- that's the essence of your --

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JOHN M. HARLAN, II
turns.text: -- due process (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: It's very -- very strongly putting here but --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: That isn't -- that is all there is to this case. That seems to be. (Inaudible)

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: All right. My -- my partner --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: What your (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: -- is going to follow me.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Are you abandoning this ground? The discharge violates the rights of the petitioner under the Constitution of the United States in that with respect to the Fifth Amendment in that it abridges his right to be free from being witness against each other? Are you abandoning that?

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: We -- we are not abandoning that. We are relying upon it and we are claiming that the discharge by the employer had the effect which Your Honor called my attention.

turns.name: CHARLES E. WHITTAKER
turns.text: Is it --

turns.name: JOHN M. HARLAN, II
turns.text: You didn't raise it.

turns.name: CHARLES E. WHITTAKER
turns.text: -- is it one thing in your view to refuse to answer a Congressional Committee under an objection of the Fifth Amendment and another thing to refuse to answer to your employer who says now you tell me what facts are?

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: That's precisely and exactly and completely our position.

turns.name: CHARLES E. WHITTAKER
turns.text: You better read Davis against the University of Kansas.

turns.name: A. L. WIRIN
turns.text: Oh, we certainly will and (Inaudible). It could be against us [Laughter].

turns.name: WM. E. LAMOREAUX
turns.text: If the Court please. I want to make one correction with reference to Globe. Counsel stated and I think inadvertently that he was a probationary employee. He was not -- he was merely a temporary employee.

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: What's the difference?

turns.name: WM. E. LAMOREAUX
turns.text: And the difference is that a temporary employee was setup in the rules to take the place of somebody who maybe absent for temporary spasmatic work. A probationer is afforded to a regular position and during a six-month period, he has -- doing which he proves himself then he becomes permanent. And Globe was -- what they call a temporary eligible employee and day to day employee might have been let go on any afternoon at 5 o'clock work thus won't let him go.

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: Is that clear as a matter of tenure or statute under California law (Inaudible) summarily dismissed at anytime?

turns.name: WM. E. LAMOREAUX
turns.text: Yes. It -- I don't know whether the -- whether in the record the rules on temporary employees, I have certified copies in here.

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: Was that ground taken by the (Inaudible)

turns.name: WM. E. LAMOREAUX
turns.text: I beg your pardon.

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: Was that ground taken by the (Inaudible)

turns.name: WM. E. LAMOREAUX
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: WM. E. LAMOREAUX
turns.text: That's right. These are temporary employees and could be let go at anytime and if they were not entitled to a hearing before the Civil Service Commission. One statement with respect to the hearing which Mr. Nelson had before the Civil Service Commission, we thought we were complying with Slochower in that kind of a hearing and that the purpose of it was for Mr. Nelson if he wished to explain why he had taken the stand he did take before the House Un-American Activities Committee. At the close of the Civil Service hearing, nobody had put on any testimony however it was -- had been these various exhibits which Mr. Wirin has referred to. At the close of the hearing, the Chairman asked Mr. Pillsbury who is Mr. Nelson's attorney whether or not there would be any witnesses. And he said at one place, “Perhaps, Mr. Nelson may wish to testify.” And then at the bottom of page 5 in the record, he said -- Mr. Pillsbury says “There would be no other witnesses.” And again on page 7, Mr. Pillsbury says “The employee does not carried or offered any evidence or testimony at this time. He merely wishes to make a statement through counsel as to his position in regard to his discharge.” And then the -- those sides rested and the counsel win their arguments.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: What was he discharged for?

turns.name: WM. E. LAMOREAUX
turns.text: He was discharged for insubordination and refusing to obey Section 1028.1 of the Government Code.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Now, getting away -- getting away from the definition of it, the legal definition, what would he be discharged for?

turns.name: WM. E. LAMOREAUX
turns.text: Charged for refusing to answer the questions --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Where?

turns.name: WM. E. LAMOREAUX
turns.text: -- certain questions before the House Un-American Activities Committee.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Where he had claimed the privilege of the Fifth Amendment?

turns.name: WM. E. LAMOREAUX
turns.text: That's correct. He claimed that. He claimed the privilege of First --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Was there any other reason that the order shows on which his discharge was based?

turns.name: WM. E. LAMOREAUX
turns.text: None of it.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: None of it.

turns.name: WM. E. LAMOREAUX
turns.text: No, they -- they can for cause.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: What was -- what was his occupation? What's --

turns.name: WM. E. LAMOREAUX
turns.text: He was a --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: -- what position was he occupied?

turns.name: WM. E. LAMOREAUX
turns.text: Medical social worker. What --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Medical social worker.

turns.name: WM. E. LAMOREAUX
turns.text: Now, what -- what that is, I don't know but that was the title of his -- his position.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Evidently a very sensitive position.

turns.name: WM. E. LAMOREAUX
turns.text: Yes. [Laughs]

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Let me ask you this question.

turns.name: WM. E. LAMOREAUX
turns.text: Yes, sir.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Can -- can a person like this employed by the County to discharge except on some statutory authority for discharge? It must -- in other words whoever is the functionary of -- purview of discharge must he have some statutory authorization.

turns.name: WM. E. LAMOREAUX
turns.text: No, Your Honor.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: What?

turns.name: WM. E. LAMOREAUX
turns.text: No, he does not.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: But --

turns.name: CHARLES E. WHITTAKER
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: WM. E. LAMOREAUX
turns.text: No, after he become a permanent tenure, he can still be discharged for -- for cause. The -- the --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: I understand that.

turns.name: WM. E. LAMOREAUX
turns.text: (Inaudible) have it but --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: What is the source of authority for discharge? Is it a statute or just --

turns.name: WM. E. LAMOREAUX
turns.text: Civil Service Rules.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Civil Service Rules.

turns.name: WM. E. LAMOREAUX
turns.text: Of the Civil Service Commission.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Well --

turns.name: CHARLES E. WHITTAKER
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: WM. E. LAMOREAUX
turns.text: I beg your pardon.

turns.name: CHARLES E. WHITTAKER
turns.text: They have the effect of law?

turns.name: WM. E. LAMOREAUX
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: CHARLES E. WHITTAKER
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: WM. E. LAMOREAUX
turns.text: Yes. They're --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: He could --

turns.name: WM. E. LAMOREAUX
turns.text: They are authorized by the charter of the County, the charter set for the Civil Service System and says that the service -- the Commission shall make rules and the rules shall provide for this and this and this and this.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: And if -- if a person who has tenure as well as protected by the Civil Service law of your State, if he's fired for a reason not sanctioned by the Civil Service law of the State, could he bring a proceeding for illegal discharge?

turns.name: WM. E. LAMOREAUX
turns.text: Well, he first -- Justice Frankfurter, the -- the (Inaudible).

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Because I don't like the cut of your hair. I don't like the crewcut.

turns.name: WM. E. LAMOREAUX
turns.text: That's true.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Now --

turns.name: WM. E. LAMOREAUX
turns.text: Then he -- within 10 days, he asked for hearing and go before the Civil Service Commission. And then the Civil Service Commission decides whether or not my boss justified and fired me because of the cut my hair.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Now, the date for justification must be in the rules or legislation. Is that right?

turns.name: WM. E. LAMOREAUX
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Therefore, you must go back to some California law to justify the charge, is that correct?

turns.name: WM. E. LAMOREAUX
turns.text: No. It will be just in the --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Well, I -- my law, I mean, Civil Service Rule that had defined (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: WM. E. LAMOREAUX
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Is that right?

turns.name: WM. E. LAMOREAUX
turns.text: But he wouldn't have to necessarily go there to find a particular reason.

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: Just -- just as the (Inaudible)

turns.name: WM. E. LAMOREAUX
turns.text: That's right.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: You mean he doesn't --

turns.name: CHARLES E. WHITTAKER
turns.text: Now --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- he -- he wouldn't give the -- he wouldn't give the authorization of such rule so and so?

turns.name: WM. E. LAMOREAUX
turns.text: No. There -- there's no -- no such thing as that to --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Well, how does the man --

turns.name: WM. E. LAMOREAUX
turns.text: (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- know when he fired within the authority of the rule or he is fired capriciously?

turns.name: WM. E. LAMOREAUX
turns.text: He --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: I don't object to the word arbitrarily so I'll use it.

turns.name: WM. E. LAMOREAUX
turns.text: The -- that's tested up before the Civil Service Commission. If that -- they will (Inaudible) and still feels aggrieved then he tries by mandate.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Now --

turns.name: WM. E. LAMOREAUX
turns.text: Now, there --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: When -- when, what's the name, Nelson was fired, was there any authorization covering his case?

turns.name: WM. E. LAMOREAUX
turns.text: The authorization covering Nelson's case was Section 1028.1 of the Government Code --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: All right. And that's the reason --

turns.name: WM. E. LAMOREAUX
turns.text: -- the state law.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: --why he was fired?

turns.name: WM. E. LAMOREAUX
turns.text: Because it says in there makes the duty of him to answer questions so that if you don't answer you're insubordinate and subject to discharge the matter provided by law.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: But you said to an answer to Justice Black's question, he was fired because he didn't answer Congressman Miller or some congressman.

turns.name: WM. E. LAMOREAUX
turns.text: That is correct.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Well, it's correct only if that comes within the provision of failure to answer insubordination. You said -- two minutes ago, you said he can be fired for insubordination in not answering questions, is that right?

turns.name: WM. E. LAMOREAUX
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: So that the --

turns.name: WM. E. LAMOREAUX
turns.text: Because it would bring to Section 1028 of the Government Code.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Well, if that's your justification, then he was quiet for not answering questions which constitute insubordination under California law.

turns.name: WM. E. LAMOREAUX
turns.text: That's right.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: All right.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: You mean then for quiet feeling to answer questions before the Committee.

turns.name: WM. E. LAMOREAUX
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: (Voice Overlap) the Committee.

turns.name: WM. E. LAMOREAUX
turns.text: Before the --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: And that's all.

turns.name: WM. E. LAMOREAUX
turns.text: That's right.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Isn't -- that is all as the California law which says that isn't subordination, isn't that right?

turns.name: WM. E. LAMOREAUX
turns.text: That's correct. The -- the -- under the California law --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: (Voice Overlap) insubordination to the federal --

turns.name: WM. E. LAMOREAUX
turns.text: -- he was insubordinate for not having answered the questions and that's why he was fired.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: And the sole basis are that -- is when he is called upon the Congressional Committee he said, “I claimed the benefit of constitutional privilege not to testify.” The California then discharged him by having claimed his privilege.

turns.name: WM. E. LAMOREAUX
turns.text: The discharge for not having answered.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Yes, for not having answered. That's right.

turns.name: CHARLES E. WHITTAKER
turns.text: (Voice Overlap) you would say (Inaudible) question. That's the thing left in the (Inaudible) and that's all the difference involved.

turns.name: WM. E. LAMOREAUX
turns.text: The questions give -- or it didn't --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Did he fail to answer?

turns.name: WM. E. LAMOREAUX
turns.text: Yes, he refused to fail -- answer the questions which were given by, I think, Mr. Taverner for the --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Did he fail to answer the other questions?

turns.name: WM. E. LAMOREAUX
turns.text: Subcommittee of the House Un-American Activities. Yes, he did. Others --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Was he discharged for failing to answer any other questions?

turns.name: WM. E. LAMOREAUX
turns.text: No, just the ones that -- which we referred -- which where I referred to when I -- in -- in the record.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Congressional Committee questions.

turns.name: WM. E. LAMOREAUX
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: And I suppose --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Can possible in it for you to make it involve of that.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Well, let me ask you a few questions too.

turns.name: WM. E. LAMOREAUX
turns.text: Yes, sir.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Let's be accurate about these things. When he was before the Civil Service Commission, if the Mass case is the ruling law of California, as I suppose it is, would he have been allowed to explain the Commission why he refused to answer the question?

turns.name: WM. E. LAMOREAUX
turns.text: Of course, he would, Your Honor.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Could he give explanation and certified answer to these questions, these and these and these unfair consequences would follow. Could he have done that?

turns.name: WM. E. LAMOREAUX
turns.text: He could have done that.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Could he explain that in detail if he choses?

turns.name: WM. E. LAMOREAUX
turns.text: He could have done, sir.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: He can give them any relevant reasons for not answering --

turns.name: WM. E. LAMOREAUX
turns.text: That's right.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- in which that Court would have had to respect.

turns.name: WM. E. LAMOREAUX
turns.text: That's right.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: And if that is -- if he has been discharged on that ground, your order would've said so, wouldn't it? Your order (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: WM. E. LAMOREAUX
turns.text: On what ground?

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: -- speaks the true (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: WM. E. LAMOREAUX
turns.text: On -- on what ground Your Honor?

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: If he had been discharged for failing to give a testimony in anyway voluntarily or involuntarily before the Civil Service Board, what was he discharged for?

turns.name: WM. E. LAMOREAUX
turns.text: He was discharged for refusing to testify before --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: When?

turns.name: WM. E. LAMOREAUX
turns.text: -- the House Un-American Activities Subcommittee.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Are you able to see from this record as a representative of California or the city of whatever it is that he was discharged for failing to give any information under any circumstances to your Civil Service Board.

turns.name: WM. E. LAMOREAUX
turns.text: No, he wasn't discharged for -- for any failure to give certain information.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Either voluntarily or involuntarily.

turns.name: WM. E. LAMOREAUX
turns.text: That's right.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Well, he was given an opportunity, was he?

turns.name: WM. E. LAMOREAUX
turns.text: He was given his opportunity to --

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: And he refused --

turns.name: WM. E. LAMOREAUX
turns.text: -- to explain.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- didn't it? And he refused, didn't it?

turns.name: WM. E. LAMOREAUX
turns.text: And he refused.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Why did he refuse?

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Page 7.

turns.name: WM. E. LAMOREAUX
turns.text: Page 7 of the record.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: And is that the reason they discharge him?

turns.name: WM. E. LAMOREAUX
turns.text: No.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: It is not, certainly not, the Board shows it.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Is that part of the record (Inaudible) read?

turns.name: WM. E. LAMOREAUX
turns.text: This is -- this is part of the record.

turns.name: FELIX FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Must we -- must we give him aid of the record and that -- read the whole of it and get the order of a commission in the same way which you get the meaning of a judgment in the light of the record.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Must -- must we in order to find this man right for the discharge, find the ground [Laughter] that your order didn't rely. Are you defending it on any ground except that he failed to testify before the Congressional Committee?

turns.name: WM. E. LAMOREAUX
turns.text: That's the only reason --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: That's the only reason --

turns.name: WM. E. LAMOREAUX
turns.text: That's the reason.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: -- the California depends on.

turns.name: WM. E. LAMOREAUX
turns.text: And because he comes squarely within the Barenblatt case.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: WM. E. LAMOREAUX
turns.text: That --

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
