turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: WM. E. LAMOREAUX
turns.text: And that there's nothing in Watkins, there is nothing in any other cases which my mind go against the way he was handled. Now, we come to Globe and our position there is that both Mass and Slochower referred to permanent employees. And as to those, they -- both cases are very firm and properly so that they should be entitled to a hearing and cannot be summarily discharged. When it comes to your temporary employee, the case did not cover that situation, that's what Globe was and our position there is that he was not entitled to a hearing and therefore his discharge was also proper.

turns.name: WILLIAM J. BRENNAN, JR.
turns.text: (Inaudible) question asked before he could have been discharged by a superior (Inaudible)

turns.name: WM. E. LAMOREAUX
turns.text: Oh, yes. That's right. As far as Globe goes, he didn't like the way he dress, the spirit of letting go.

turns.name: FRED OKRAND
turns.text: May it please the Court. I'd like to address myself first with reference to the Globe discharge which was the discharge that Mr. Lamoreaux concluded on. Our position is that the mere fact that Mr. Globe was a temporary employee, it doesn't dispose of this case. Mr. Justice Brennan asked if he could be discharged for any reason because he was temporary employees and Mr. Lamoreaux answered yes. We suggest that the Constitution doesn't go that far. There is no dispute in this record that he was discharged for refusal to answer before the House Committee, no dispute at all.

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: (Inaudible) discharge --

turns.name: FRED OKRAND
turns.text: Now --

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: -- for -- for having his haircut --

turns.name: FRED OKRAND
turns.text: No.

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: FRED OKRAND
turns.text: We say not. And we say we have authority for that in the Wieman case where this Court repeated what it had been said in the Mitchell case that Congress could not pass a law which says that no Jew, Republican or Negro could be employed. Now, Congress can't do that. The County of Los Angeles couldn't do it nor could the County of Los Angeles have discharge a temporary employee for being a Jew, a Republican or Negro. And we say that the County of Los Angeles cannot discharge a temporary employee for merely refusing to answer on proper constitutional ground before the (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: Are the Civil --

turns.name: CHARLES E. WHITTAKER
turns.text: Now --

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: -- Service regulations in the record?

turns.name: FRED OKRAND
turns.text: They are not.

turns.name: CHARLES E. WHITTAKER
turns.text: What are the constitutional grounds (Inaudible) what prohibits one from discharging another when no reason at all admittedly need be given.

turns.name: FRED OKRAND
turns.text: We think there may be a difference in a case where no reason may be given and a case where it's clearer that the reason that is given is an unconstitutional reason.

turns.name: CHARLES E. WHITTAKER
turns.text: In other words, though you didn't have to give it all, it'd be given bad when you (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: FRED OKRAND
turns.text: Right. If you discharge a person in violation of the Constitution, you can't do that. That's the -- as we read Wieman and that's as we read Slochower. We just -- we think that the Due Process Clause of the Fourteenth Amendment protects the citizen from arbitrary conduct by his government. And it -- to go a little further, it protects an applicant to a job from arbitrary conduct by his government and in this case, it protects an employee from arbitrary discharge by his government.

turns.name: JOHN M. HARLAN, II
turns.text: Well, they've fired provided that they didn't give any reason.

turns.name: FRED OKRAND
turns.text: Well, if -- if they -- if we -- if they didn't give a reason but even if we could prove, if we could prove that it was unconstitutional reason, we would go that far and say that the Due Process Clause would protect him. Usually, it can't prove --

turns.name: JOHN M. HARLAN, II
turns.text: Tell me, did you raise or didn't you the privileges immunities point? I understood --

turns.name: FRED OKRAND
turns.text: Well, as Mr. Wirin --

turns.name: JOHN M. HARLAN, II
turns.text: -- your partner (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: FRED OKRAND
turns.text: -- said this is embarrassing, we can raise that. We raise it in our petition. It's clear there. It's very clear as clear as any lawyer ought to make it, but, Your Honor, we did not press it in the appellate courts.

turns.name: JOHN M. HARLAN, II
turns.text: All right. I see.

turns.name: FRED OKRAND
turns.text: So it's in the record in the sense that we raised it but we did not press it in the appellate courts.

turns.name: JOHN M. HARLAN, II
turns.text: Is there at large incidence?

turns.name: FRED OKRAND
turns.text: It's there.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: FRED OKRAND
turns.text: We hope so, we think so. We think so.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: FRED OKRAND
turns.text: Well, it's -- it's there in black and white in this record raised by us. But we -- we can't tell this Court that we urged upon the District Court of Appeal or even upon the Supreme Court on petition for hearing.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: FRED OKRAND
turns.text: Yes. We are present. We're permitted to press it, we do press it. I see I'm through just one more thing on this record 7. Let me just say this, Your Honor. In context, well, Mr. Pillsbury said, the employee does not care to offer any evidence or testimony as this time. What he said was and what he meant was the employee does not care to offer any more evidence at this time. He had already offered evidence showing his fitness for employment. So that's the way this sentence should be read.

turns.name: CHARLES E. WHITTAKER
turns.text: (Inaudible) if I may. One last question (Inaudible) what would the (Inaudible) have said Nelson at this hearing that would have been a defense?

turns.name: FRED OKRAND
turns.text: I don't know.

turns.name: CHARLES E. WHITTAKER
turns.text: Would it have been a defense to have answered categorically the questions which he has said to have refused to answer to the House Committee?

turns.name: FRED OKRAND
turns.text: We have no construction of that face of the case.

turns.name: CHARLES E. WHITTAKER
turns.text: Well, you have the statute.

turns.name: FRED OKRAND
turns.text: The statute --

turns.name: CHARLES E. WHITTAKER
turns.text: What the statute say?

turns.name: FRED OKRAND
turns.text: -- the statute would say he had to be filed.(Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: CHARLES E. WHITTAKER
turns.text: The statute says all he can do is explain why he did what he did some place else.

turns.name: FRED OKRAND
turns.text: Well, the statute doesn't say that.

turns.name: JOHN M. HARLAN, II
turns.text: (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: CHARLES E. WHITTAKER
turns.text: Well, then, it --

turns.name: FRED OKRAND
turns.text: The Mass case puts that kind of a gloss on.

turns.name: JOHN M. HARLAN, II
turns.text: There is a strong implication or intimation or whatever you chose to call for the Mass case that despite the statute on its face --

turns.name: FRED OKRAND
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JOHN M. HARLAN, II
turns.text: -- an explanation might be a defense.

turns.name: FRED OKRAND
turns.text: That's right.

turns.name: JOHN M. HARLAN, II
turns.text: You -- you agree with that.

turns.name: FRED OKRAND
turns.text: I agree with that. We say however that the Speiser case says that they can't put that burden on us within the Due Process Clause.

turns.name: CHARLES E. WHITTAKER
turns.text: Now, in -- and I can read. Here is Section 1907 in the Civil Service Rules of the County of Los Angeles it's says, “Such an employee shall be entitled to answer, explain or deny the charge rules in writing within 10 business days but shall not be entitled to a hearing except in the case of fraud etcetera.”

turns.name: FRED OKRAND
turns.text: That's --

turns.name: CHARLES E. WHITTAKER
turns.text: Now --

turns.name: FRED OKRAND
turns.text: -- first to a temporary employee, Your Honor.

turns.name: CHARLES E. WHITTAKER
turns.text: That's a temporary employee.

turns.name: FRED OKRAND
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: CHARLES E. WHITTAKER
turns.text: But he have a different rule with respect to permanent --

turns.name: FRED OKRAND
turns.text: Well, the -- the permanent employee has a right to a full hearing. That's the distinct -- that -- they only cite that to show that a temporary employee has no right to a hearing.

turns.name: CHARLES E. WHITTAKER
turns.text: Now, I see. All right.

turns.name: HUGO L. BLACK
turns.text: I presume that might mean that he's (Inaudible) to deny that he refused to answer (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: FRED OKRAND
turns.text: Yes. He's entitled to, he didn't though.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Thank you, gentlemen.
